text.title: GitOps — Secrets and Security Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules including a `skip-this-methodology` rule. Each pairs a binary check with sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Secrets MUST NEVER appear as plaintext in Git. Use SOPS-encrypted manifests, Sealed Secrets, or External Secrets Operator (ESO).
rule.rationale: Plaintext secrets in Git are the #1 leak class — once committed, presume compromised.

rule.statement: Encryption keys MUST be backed by cloud KMS (AWS KMS / GCP KMS / Azure Key Vault) OR an audited age keyring. Storing the encryption key in the same repo is rejected.
rule.rationale: If both ciphertext and key live in Git, encryption is theatre; key custody must be external.

rule.statement: Each secret class MUST have a documented rotation cadence (e.g. tokens 90d, passwords 180d, certs 365d) with automation for at least token rotation.
rule.rationale: Static secrets accumulate exposure; rotation caps the time window of compromise.

rule.statement: ServiceAccounts MUST be scoped to the minimum verbs + resources required. ClusterRole/*/* assignments rejected outside infra controllers.
rule.rationale: Broad RBAC turns one compromised pod into cluster takeover; least privilege caps blast radius.

rule.statement: AI agents operating on the GitOps repo MUST follow an explicit secret policy: read decrypted secrets only when required, never log them, never echo to chat. Policy MUST be enforced via guardrails.
rule.rationale: AI agents are a new exfil class — they can read, transform, and surface secrets across contexts; explicit policy + guardrails are mandatory.

rule.statement: Skip when secrets never live in manifests (CSI-injected) OR single-dev cluster with no compliance obligation.
rule.rationale: Below threshold the methodology adds ceremony without risk reduction.
